speaker: interviewer
text: You sat around a table with some director of a factory, a doctor, so-and-so-- Mrs. Lyon, I think we would like to continue within Ravensbruck, where we were talking about coupons and how they were issued and to whom they were issued.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. The SS wanted to increase the volume of work, and in order to do this, they offered the French women and Polish and Danish-- these political prisoners that-- who were not Jewish-- coupons. These coupons had a certain value, so-- so that if they completed-- finished a certain amount of work, they could get coupons valued at a certain amount. They could then-- the inmates then could take these coupons to a little canteen-- canteen and then they could obtain or purchase food with it, drink as well. Now, I never saw the canteen, but apparently it did exist. However, the French resisted. They resisted, and they started getting beaten up by the-- the-- the SS beat them up and cussed them out-- and-- and you just don't want to work hard, you lazy bums, and accused them of all sorts of things-- for-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: The French women then decided, and the others, that, why should they get beaten up? So they'll accept the coupons, but they're not going to buy anything with it. And this became a-- a resistance type of movement. Actually, it became resistance.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were going to-- they realized that if they are able to do more work while their stomachs have some food in it, then we, the Jews, will not be able to keep up with them. And we'll-- we'll be in a less tolerable position. So it's really an act of kindness that-- that these women have acted the way they-- they did. They could have accepted these coupons and just cared for themselves. But no, they-- they were up to their true selves for the reasons that they were arrested and helped us, the Jewish prisoners. But of course, they helped themselves, as well, because then they did not have to rush, rush, rush all the time, but they continued to go at a pretty good pace anyway, which all of us have to do. So I'm grateful to the-- particularly the French prisoners who-- who backed us so loyally.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was wondering if we could go back just a little bit--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to the Continental Gummi-Werke. I was just wondering, you did speak to some people who lived there during the time. But did you, on your return visits, speak to any of the people who are running the company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, thank you for reminding me. Indeed, I forgot that part. Oh, yes. So the time came for me to visit the Continental Gummi-Werke, and the general manager of engineering took-- took us around the premises, the huge yard. And I remembered exactly where our camp was located, and I told him that the electric wire fences were over there, and that little church, where actually I met their board of directors who invited me to this event at the factory, because they heard me speak at the unveiling of this monument. And I just learned that the Continental Gummi-Werke still does not recognize the fact that there were slave laborers. And I said, well, it's just in time. So I gave a little speech. And in it, I said, that I understand that the Continental Gummi-Werke to date does not acknowledge the fact that they had slave laborers during the war, but I was one who was a slave laborer here, at the Continental Gummi-Werke, at the age of 15, and that I would like to visit the factory while I am in Hannover, whether I am invited or not. And apparently they had their spies there, and-- and while we had a little reception in that church, which we could see from the grounds of the factory-- I remembered very well. It was very charming-- charming church. And it's a very old, historical church. And I am so glad that that is where the little reception was held for us in Hannover. And so it was subsequent to that. That evening, at the reception, three people came up to me that they are from the board of directors of the Continental Gummi-Werke. They heard me speak, and they'd like to invite me to the factory. And I said, well, but we are here on a mission. We are filming for a documentary film, and so, where I go, my husband goes. And the crew says, certainly, we understand. And they are welcome. And I said, and Dr. [? Fulberg ?] Stuhlberg also has to come because he helps me translate. And they didn't like it at first, but then they consented, and he came along. And-- and in the final film-- well, you will see us in the board of directors room where I ask the general manager of engineering--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name? Do we want to mention his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see, his name is-- I know it so well. And I-- may I give it to you later? I'm sorry, I can't recall it momentarily. Doctor-- hmm. I'm sorry. But it is at that board of directors room where I'm asking-- where I asked him to please share information with me of this-- this period. I need to unravel my-- well, find out information about my-- my history, particularly during the Holocaust period. And would he please help me? And he said, I-- I'd like to very much, but I don't know that we have such records. And-- and-- I wanted to see something, something that I can put my finger on, some kind of record with my name on it, since I was pulling together the odds and ends, and everything was very difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: But some things I found, others I didn't, and this gentleman-- either he was telling the truth or-- and he didn't really know, but he said that the right person to go to would be a Dr. Von Herz. He's the public relations director. So eventually, that's what-- that's what we wanted to do. But while we were still talking about my needs to find records, Dr. [? Fulberg ?] Stuhlberg asked the general manager of engineering, well, don't you even have records in your secret archives?

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, this took him by surprise, and he said, I don't know anything about a secret-- about secret archives but if-- if there is, of course, we'll find out, and certainly we'll cooperate with you. To make a long story short, nothing came of our meeting. I have corresponded with Dr. [? Fulberg ?] Stuhlberg, and he wrote kind letters. However, this factory was left unrecognized. They had another branch to the factory where they manufactured [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] other material and that-- Stocken-- and I think it was batteries for submarines, and a lot of people died. A lot of the inmates died from lead poisoning in that factory and the factory could-- officials could see that there was no way out and that they have to recognize it, and so they, with the blessings-- well, the city put up the monument, and they cooperated in doing so, and I also went to that unveiling. It was very, very moving.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned the word Stocken.

speaker: interviewee
text: Stocken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in Hannover. It's-- It's not Linden-Limmer, it's a different area called Stocken.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is this Continental Gummi-Werke being utilized today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it is all over the world. I was in Mexico, I saw-- I saw them there. They are making rubber tires, the biggest in Europe in rubber tires, and they are making rubber accessories for refrigerators, washing machines, and other appliances, and they are still making gas masks. I went there, and I saw this man pull out a-- a gas mask, and it was still yellow, well it-- like chartreuse yellow, greenish yellow, and hot to the touch and was cooling and turning dark green in my hand. And I thought, times really have changed and modern machines. And I recall how we used to do it from thin sheet rubber and a form. It was totally different. And these things were so heavy, and the slave laborers had to lift them. And-- it's totally different now. It-- it seemed like Kinderspiel, or children's play, compared to the process that existed during World War II. As a young girl of 15, I-- I-- [? sometimes-- ?] I really experience many, many things. As you can see, one can only talk about a segment of these experiences. And if I remember this much, I-- I must have experienced much more. But thank god for the lapse of time, for its healing. I forget more and more and it is good. It is good. It's important to remember the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we get back a little bit to your leaving Ravensbruck? I don't think we finished Ravensbruck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and actually, I'm sort of commenting on the-- on the total area, that I want to be able to tell you many more details, and I'm sure, that after this interview is over with, I will think of lots of other examples. But as of this moment, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Ravensbruck was a camp, to me, when I was the weakest in my entire life, when I was totally emaciated, and I was suffering from-- from malnutrition. My right leg was completely full of holes, full of deep holes that refused to heal. It started really just before we-- I went to Beendorf, already. But there, it was just little pimples. First a couple, then five, six, and then 10 or 12, and then they would fuse together and would become larger and deeper, and they would become infected, and we had no medical care, whatsoever. But I always felt that we must go on. We just can't give up. I must let mother know that I am alive. She really was my magnetic force. I somehow always felt that I must let her know that I am still here, I'm still struggling, and that if I only know that she is alive still, I will continue my struggle. You know, if I had learned anything negative about my mother, I-- I would have given up, just like Anne Frank. I would have probably given up like she, in Bergen-Belsen. She gave off when her sister contracted typhus, and she was all alone. She, too, was in Auschwitz, and she was shipped to Bergen-Belsen. By the way, in research I found that she was about six months older than I was at the time, when we were in Auschwitz, and that she went to Bergen-Belsen in October or November-- anyway, a few weeks before I did. And-- but when her sister, Margot, died, she could no longer-- she could no longer go on. And-- and she probably caught it herself, but this I'm not sure of. But it was very easy to give up and catch many things. I sometimes wonder how come that I made it. In these train rides, I'd be sitting on top of dead people who died of typhus, tuberculosis, or other serious ailments, and I came out without even-- without any scars in this area. What I have, however-- the long-term effect of the camp, is perhaps the way I should put it, was that I suffered from malnutrition and consequently, my shoulders did not grow properly. And years later, first with the left arm, I couldn't even raise my arm to comb my hair, and so-- and then my right arm. And it had-- this had to be corrected through surgery. Tests showed that the head of the humerus was rubbing against the acromion process. There should be some room for-- for-- some room between the two to articulate, but there wasn't, and when a dye test was injected, there was only one fourth of the room of a normal shoulder. Consequently, the top of my shoulder bone, which is the acromion process, had to be cut down, cut back, so that it won't rub against the bone, but-- and the deltoid muscle, which controls our arm, had to be lifted, relocated. They drilled a hole further over, and through the shavings of the bone, they replanted the deltoid muscle. And then, through many months of help, I was able to start lifting up my arm. It's not as easy as it would normally be, but can-- I can now do it. Three years later, my right arm gave me the same problem. It was, again, relieved by the same process. And my spine was affected. I had three laminectomy operations. Both-- all of them were split-- the bone splintered-- and then two cervical laminectomies, all for malnutrition suffered in my early youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was very difficult to find a neurosurgeon who identified the problem. It finally took an old gentleman, Dr. Cox, who was a doctor's doctor. He was the seventh doctor that our neurosurgeon called in, a very fine doctor, who finally identified the problem. But he, I remember, scratched his head and he said, last time I saw something like that was during the Depression, but where would you have suffered from malnutrition? I said, oh, Dr. Cox, do I have a story to tell you. And of course, then he wrote a three or four page single-spaced, typewritten letter to all the other doctors, to give them instructions on this type of problem.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very interesting. And in the meantime, I kept telling Dr. [? Mathis, ?] who is my neurosurgeon-- Jacob [? Mathis ?] in San Francisco, a fine neurosurgeon, who operated me everywhere. And I said, doctor, it feels as though my bone is rubbing against bone. He says, oh, Gloria, if that were the case, you wouldn't be able to talk to me or you would be in such pain. I said, doctor, that's what I'm trying to tell you. I am in a lot of pain, and I can't lie down at night. It was-- it's excruciatingly painful. And then he finally realized what was happening. Of course, we didn't have all the equipment that we have today that one could really find out much easier as to what the problem is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we continue with your experiences in Ravensbruck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my seventh camp was at Ravensbruck and from there we were shipped out quite suddenly, as usual-- at night, in cattle cars. And that train moved for three days, just moved and stopped, moved and stopped. Apparently quite aimlessly. At long last, we arrived at an open meadow and there the-- we were kept locked in the cattle cars, and we heard the-- in German-- voices outside that here they're going to shoot us. We were all going to die, and we just couldn't believe that it would end like this. About 50%, at least 50%, of the women with me were dead, and we were sitting on them. And finally, they asked-- they-- they opened the doors and-- of the cattle car and said, push out the dead and the sick. And we couldn't get ourselves to hurt the sick anymore, so we just-- if they're going to shoot us, let them. Let at least the-- the sick live a little longer, and we didn't touch the sick, not in our cattle car. I don't know what they did in the others. And we got off, and it was this tall grass and the line was so long. I didn't know what was happening, but we had to go in a certain direction and it moved very, very slowly. And by this time it was April 1945, and we had no idea how the war is coming along. I just know that the German cities where I was, was being pounded to pulp. And, of course, as prisoners, we-- we really wanted to die rather from a bomb than starve to death or beaten to death, and that's-- that's the only time in life that I ever had to-- well, made a decision that, if I'm gonna die, it's going to be from an air raid attack. Well, maybe I'll be in a train and-- or maybe I'll be out in the field, and they'll shoot us, but it's not gonna be from starvation. It's a form of fighting [? weapon ?] but of course, it probably would have been starvation. There was no-- but we were trying to deny it, I think, in some way. So we were walking through the tall, mushy grass. It was wet and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] finally, at the very end, when I reached a certain point, I saw some people-- a woman in front of me raised her skirt. And-- and they put something into her skirt from burlap sacks. There were two burlap sacks. Finally, I saw that one was filled with raw macaroni and-- raw macaroni. And the other one was filled with sugar. And one woman took a handful of macaroni and plopped it into her skirt or dress, and-- and the same thing with just plain sugar. That's odd, I thought. All this time in the camp, and-- camps, I've never seen anything like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would they feed us, anyway, if they're gonna shoot us? It-- it-- it just didn't-- didn't make sense. But I decided, I'm going to hold up my skirt, too-- or my dress, it wasn't a skirt, it was a gray dress-- and kept looking over there, anxiously, and they plopped these items into my dress. And lo and behold, I had no idea that they had holes in, them and the macaroni and sugar just spilled on the tall, mushy grass.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what to do, what to do. So I decided to quickly pick up the raw macaroni, at least. The sugar was a total loss. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at that point, I was beaten unconscious for holding up the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, what doesn't add up is, they were going to shoot us, then they gave us something to eat, and then-- were going to give us something to eat and-- and then-- and then, to beat up anybody-- if they want us to live, then, why-- why would they beat me up? Nothing made sense in the camp. There were so many contradictions that there's no way that I can even-- I can explain it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no rhyme or reason for this or that. We just had to accept what was dished out to us, I mean physically and in every other respect, as well. So I really would have died there had-- had not some of my fellow prisoners still been capable of human feelings and compassion. I was told later that they half-dragged and half carried me back to the box car. I came to when I heard a voice, a man's voice. And it said, we are-- you are lucky, he said. It was one of our German guards, but we were already on a regular train. It was not a boxcar. I have no idea how I-- how I got on it or where I got on it. For many, many years, until we had a reunion a few years ago in Ravensbruck, I used to think-- well, this is what I remembered until then. That-- that this guard, I remember seeing him. He said that you are lucky because you are free, but I have to turn around and go back and face the music. And I thought, that's strange. No, they were about to kill me. They were about to-- they were about to feed me, then they were about to kill me again, and now they telling me that we are free. I was totally confused, and of course, I was in terrible shape. [? To-- ?] I hadn't eaten for several days. A-- a few years ago, I learned that we were on the road, picking up to different camps nearby for four days, and-- and only then did the following happen, and this I remember. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came to when this man said that we-- we are lucky, that we are free. And I-- I really was surprised. And the women around me were fewer people and some new faces, and they said, yes, we are traveling through the Danish countryside, that soon will be in Copenhagen. And soon-- sure enough, pretty soon, the train pulled into a beautiful station in beautiful Copenhagen, and church bells were ringing, welcoming us, the-- and the civilians-- civilians, I shouldn't call them as if I was military-- but-- but the-- the population was behind barricades. But they broke through the barricades, and they were waving little Danish flags, and they came up to the train, which they were not supposed to because many of us suffered from typhus and tuberculosis and other serious ailments, and so they came up to the train and they handed us brown bags.

speaker: interviewer
text: And mine had peeled, boiled potatoes, cut up, still warm to the touch. And also, chocolate. When did I see chocolate last? Certainly not during my incarceration. And there were wonderful things in it. I can't remember all the detail, but the chocolate made a big hit.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I just didn't-- I didn't eat. I put my tongue on it, perhaps, but I didn't eat anything. The women, however, most of them began to eat and eat and eat, and they became dreadfully ill from over-eating. Our stomachs were shrunken so much that they could not handle food anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: End of tape eight.
